en.extract:
The Barolo shearwater (Puffinus baroli), also known as the North Atlantic little shearwater or Macaronesian shearwater, is a small shearwater which breeds in the Azores and Canaries of Macaronesia in the North Atlantic Ocean.  The English name and the specific baroli refers to Carlo Tencredi Falletti, marquis of Barolo.


Taxonomy
Barolo shearwater was formally described in 1857 by the French naturalist Charles Lucien Bonaparte under the binomial name Procellaria baroli. The specific epithet commemorates Carlo Tencredi Falletti, marquis of Barolo. This shearwater is now placed in the genus Puffinus that was introduced by the French zoologist Mathurin Jacques Brisson in 1760 with the Manx shearwater (Puffinus puffinus) as the type species. The species in monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.
It was previously considered conspecific with the little shearwater (Puffinus assimilis) of the southern hemisphere. Mitochondrial DNA cytochrome b sequence analysis indicated that baroli and boydi were very close to the nominate subspecies of the Audubon's shearwater complex, now known as Sargasso shearwater (P. lherminieri). BirdLife International retain the forms baroli and boydi within little shearwater. The British Ornithologists' Union  accepted P. baroli as a distinct species in 2005, as has Clements Checklist.  The American Ornithologists' Union followed in 2013.


Description
The Barolo shearwater is visually similar to the Manx shearwater and other North Atlantic Puffinus, but can be distinguished by features such as a pale face with a darkly contrasting eye, a silvery panel in the upperwings, shorter more rounded wings, and blue feet.


Distribution
The Barolo shearwater breeds on the Azores, Desertas, Savage and Canary Islands. The largest colony, consisting of 1400 pairs, occurs on the Selvagen Islands. The non-breeding range is the tropical and sub-tropic northeast Atlantic.


Behaviour
The Barolo shearwater feeds in the upper 15m of the water column, which is similar to the closely related Sargasso shearwater Puffinus lherminieri of the western Atlantic and Caribbean Sea. Barolo shearwaters do not have a preferred time of day to forage or rest and they may hunt for food during either day or night, although they seem to be more ready to fly in the daylight hours. They feed mainly on fish and cephalopods, with Argonauta argo being the most common cephalopod taken in the Azores, while the fish taken were almost exclusively from the genus Phycis.


Threats
Like other Procellariforms, introduced predators, such as rats and cats, are their main threat at breeding colonies. In addition, fledglings are attracted to artificial lights at night during their maiden flights from nests to the sea. On Tenerife,  in the Canary Islands, a decline on the number of birds attracted to lights have been reported, suggesting a population decline on the island.


References


External links
BTO BirdFacts - Macaronesian shearwater
Madeira Birds: Macaronesian shearwater
Recordings of Barolo shearwater at Xeno-Canto

The '''Barolo shearwater''' ('''''Puffinus baroli'''''), also known as the '''North Atlantic little shearwater''' or '''Macaronesian shearwater''', is a small [[shearwater]] which breeds in the [[Azores]] and [[Canary Islands|Canaries]] of [[Macaronesia]] in the [[North Atlantic Ocean]].  The English name and the specific ''baroli'' refers to [[Carlo Tancredi Falletti di Barolo|Carlo Tencredi Falletti]], marquis of [[Barolo, Piedmont|Barolo]].

==Taxonomy==
Barolo shearwater was [[species description|formally described]] in 1857 by the French naturalist [[Charles Lucien Bonaparte]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Procellaria baroli''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Bonaparte | first=Charles Lucien | author-link=Charles Lucien Bonaparte | year=1857 | title=Conspectus Generum Avium | volume=2 | language=Latin | location=Lugduni Batavorum | publisher=Apud E.J. Brill | page=204 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/43549765 }}</ref> The specific epithet commemorates [[Carlo Tancredi Falletti di Barolo|Carlo Tencredi Falletti]], marquis of [[Barolo, Piedmont|Barolo]].<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=67 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n67/mode/1up }}</ref> This shearwater is now placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Puffinus]]'' that was introduced by the French zoologist [[Mathurin Jacques Brisson]] in 1760 with the [[Manx shearwater]] (''Puffinus puffinus'') as the [[type species]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Brisson | first=Mathurin Jacques | author-link=Mathurin Jacques Brisson | year=1760 | title=Ornithologie, ou, Méthode Contenant la Division des Oiseaux en Ordres, Sections, Genres, Especes & leurs Variétés | language=fr, la | at=[https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36010456 Vol. 1, p. 56], [https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36210979 Vol. 6, pp. 129-130] | place=Paris | publisher=Jean-Baptiste Bauche }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=January 2022 | title=Petrels, albatrosses | work=IOC World Bird List Version 12.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/petrels/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=19 February 2022 }}</ref> The species in [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>

It was previously considered [[conspecific]] with the [[little shearwater]] (''Puffinus assimilis'') of the [[Southern Hemisphere|southern hemisphere]]. [[Mitochondrial DNA]] [[cytochrome b|cytochrome ''b'']] [[DNA sequence|sequence]] analysis indicated that ''baroli'' and ''boydi'' were very close to the [[nominate subspecies]] of the Audubon's shearwater complex,<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Austin | first1=J.J. | last2=Bretagnolle | first2=V. | last3=Pasquet | first3=E. | date=2004 | title=A global molecular phylogeny of the small ''Puffinus'' shearwaters and implications for systematics of the little-Audubon's shearwater complex | journal=The Auk | jstor=4090321 | volume=121 | issue=3 | pages=847–864 | doi=10.1642/0004-8038(2004)121[0847:AGMPOT]2.0.CO;2 }}</ref> now known as [[Sargasso shearwater]] (''P. lherminieri''). [[BirdLife International]] retain the forms ''baroli'' and ''boydi'' within little shearwater. The [[British Ornithologists' Union]]  accepted ''P. baroli'' as a distinct species in 2005,<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Sangster | first1=G. | last2=Collinson | first2=J.M. | last3=Helbig | first3=A.J. | last4=Knox | first4=A.G. | last5=Parkin | first5=D.T. | date=2005 | title=Taxonomic recommendations for British birds: third report | journal=Ibis | volume=147 | issue=4 | pages=821–826 | doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.2005.00483.x | doi-access=free }}</ref> as has ''[[Clements Checklist]]''.  The [[American Ornithologists' Union]] followed in 2013.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Chesser | first1=R.T. | last2=Banks | first2=R.C. | last3=Barker | first3=F.K. | last4=Cicero | first4=C. | last5=Dunn | first5=J.L. | last6=Kratter | first6=A.W. | last7=Lovette | first7=I.J. | last8=Rasmussen | first8=P.C. | last9=Remsen | first9=J.V.J. | last10=Rising | first10=J.D. | last11=Stotz | first11=D.F. | last12=Winker | first12=K. | date=2013 | title=Fifty-Fourth supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union Check-list of North American Birds | journal=The Auk | volume=130 | issue=3 | pages=558–571 | doi=10.1525/auk.2013.130.3.1 | doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Description==
The Barolo shearwater is visually similar to the [[Manx shearwater]] and other North Atlantic ''[[Puffinus]]'', but can be distinguished by features such as a pale face with a darkly contrasting eye, a silvery panel in the upperwings, shorter more rounded wings, and blue feet.<ref>McGeehan & Mullarney 1995, Martin & Rowlands 2001.</ref><ref name = WindBirds>{{cite web | url = http://www.madeirabirds.com/barolo-shearwater-puffinus-baroli | title = Barolo's Shearwater ''Puffinus baroli'' (Bonaparte, 1857) | accessdate = 10 December 2016 | publisher =  Wind Birds, Lda| date = 2005-10-07 }}</ref>

==Distribution==
The Barolo shearwater breeds on the [[Azores]], [[Desertas]], [[Savage Islands|Savage]] and [[Canary Islands]].<ref name=bow>{{cite journal | last1=Kirwan | first1=G.M. | last2=Carboneras | first2=C. | last3=Jutglar | first3=F. | date=2020 | title=Barolo Shearwater (''Puffinus baroli''), version 1.0 | editor1-last=Billerman | editor1-first=S.M. | editor2-last=Keeney | editor2-first=B.K. | editor3-last=Rodewald | editor3-first=P.G. | editor4-last=Schulenberg | editor4-first=T.S. | journal=Birds of the World | location=Ithaca, NY, USA | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | doi=10.2173/bow.litshe1.01 | s2cid=241706768 | url=https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.litshe1.01 | access-date=19 February 2022 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> The largest colony, consisting of 1400 pairs, occurs on the Selvagen Islands.<ref name = "Birds & Company">{{cite web | url = http://www.birdsandcompany.com/en/macaronesian-shearwater-puffinus-baroli/ | title = Macaronesian Shearwater (Barolo Shearwater) | accessdate = 10 December 2016 | publisher = www.birdsandcompany.com}}</ref> The non-breeding range is the tropical and sub-tropic northeast Atlantic.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Ramos | first1=R. | last2=Paiva | first2=V.H. | last3=Zajková | first3=Z. | last4=Precheur | first4=C. | last5=Fagundes | first5=A.I. | last6=Jodice | first6=P.G.R. | last7=Mackin | first7=W. | last8=Zino | first8=F. | last9=Bretagnolle | first9=V. | last10=González-Solís | first10=J. | date=2021 | title=Spatial ecology of closely related taxa: the case of the little shearwater complex in the North Atlantic Ocean | journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society | volume=191 | issue=2 | pages=482–502 | doi=10.1093/zoolinnean/zlaa045 | doi-access=free | hdl=10261/228870 | hdl-access=free }}</ref>

==Behaviour==
The Barolo shearwater feeds in the upper 15m of the water column, which is similar to the closely related [[Sargasso shearwater]] ''Puffinus lherminieri'' of the western Atlantic and [[Caribbean Sea]]. Barolo shearwaters do not have a preferred time of day to forage or rest and they may hunt for food during either day or night, although they seem to be more ready to fly in the daylight hours. They feed mainly on fish and [[cephalopod]]s, with ''[[Argonauta argo]]'' being the most common cephalopod taken in the Azores, while the fish taken were almost exclusively from the genus ''[[Phycis]]''.<ref name=Neves>{{Cite journal | last1=Neves | first1=V.C. | last2=Bried | first2=J. | last3=González-Solís | first3=J. | last4=Roscales | first4=J.L. | last5=Clarke | first5=M.R. | date=2012 | title=Feeding ecology and movements of the Barolo shearwater ''Puffinus baroli baroli'' in the Azores, NE Atlantic | journal=Marine Ecology Progress Series | volume=452 | pages=269–285 | doi=10.3354/meps09670 | doi-access=free | bibcode=2012MEPS..452..269N | hdl=2445/61235 | hdl-access=free }}</ref>

==Threats==
Like other Procellariforms, introduced predators, such as rats and cats, are their main threat at breeding colonies. In addition, fledglings are attracted to artificial lights at night during their maiden flights from nests to the sea.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Rodríguez|first1=Airam|title=Attraction of petrels to artificial lights in the Canary Islands: effects of the moon phase and age class|journal=Ibis|date=2009|volume=151|issue=2|pages=299–310|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.2009.00925.x|hdl=10261/45133|hdl-access=free}}</ref> On Tenerife,  in the Canary Islands, a decline on the number of birds attracted to lights have been reported, suggesting a population decline on the island.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Rodríguez|first1=Airam|title=Trends in numbers of petrels attracted to artificial lights suggest population declines in Tenerife, Canary Islands|journal=Ibis|date=2012|volume=154|pages=167–172|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.2011.01175.x|hdl=10261/45113|hdl-access=free}}</ref>
